attributes.stem:
A 64-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department 12 hours after undergoing a cystoscopy and dilatation of a posterior urethral stricture. He has developed rigors and confusion. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell and confused. His pulse rate is 106/min, blood pressure 80/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 39.6°C. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. A urinary catheter is in situ. Supplemental oxygen has been commenced via facemask.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Rapid infusion of 500 mL 0.9% sodium chloride solution
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with septic shock, likely secondary to a urinary tract infection following a urological procedure. The patient's hypotension, tachycardia, fever, and altered mental status are all indicative of severe sepsis or septic shock.

Rapid infusion of 500 mL 0.9% sodium chloride solution is the most appropriate next step in management. In the initial management of septic shock, rapid fluid resuscitation is crucial to improve tissue perfusion and maintain organ function. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommend immediate fluid resuscitation with 30 mL/kg of crystalloid solution within the first three hours for patients with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion.

Blood cultures are indeed important in the management of sepsis, as they can guide antibiotic therapy. However, they are not the immediate priority in this case. Blood cultures should be obtained before starting antibiotics, if possible, but should not delay fluid resuscitation or antibiotic administration.

Arterial blood gas analysis can provide valuable information about oxygenation, ventilation, and metabolic status in sepsis. However, it is not the most urgent step in this scenario. A venous blood gas could provide similar information about the patient's acid-base status and lactate level, which is an important marker of tissue hypoperfusion in sepsis.

Removal of urinary catheter may be necessary as part of source control in urinary sepsis. However, it is not the immediate priority in this critically ill patient. The catheter might also be useful for monitoring urine output during resuscitation.

Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics are a crucial part of sepsis management and should be administered as soon as possible, ideally within one hour of recognition of sepsis. However, in this case, rapid fluid resuscitation takes precedence as the patient is in shock. Antibiotics should follow immediately after initial fluid resuscitation has begun.

After initial fluid resuscitation, the next steps would include starting broad-spectrum antibiotics, obtaining blood cultures and other relevant investigations, considering vasopressors if hypotension persists despite fluid resuscitation, and addressing the source of infection, which may include removing or changing the urinary catheter.
learningPoints: Rapid fluid resuscitation with crystalloids is the first step in managing septic shock.
Early recognition and treatment of sepsis is crucial to prevent progression to septic shock and organ dysfunction.
Urinary tract procedures can lead to post-operative sepsis, especially in patients with urethral strictures.
references: Evans L, Rhodes A, Alhazzani W, Antonelli M, Coopersmith CM, French C et al. Surviving sepsis campaign: international guidelines for management of sepsis and septic shock. Intensive care medicine. 2021;47(11):1181-1247. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34599691/
Sepsis (for EDs and UCCs). In: Safer Care Victoria [online]. 2022. Available at: https://www.safercare.vic.gov.au/best-practice-improvement/clinical-guidance/emergency/sepsis
